hook: The last great ducal house in Greater London still in family hands, hiding Robert Adam's first masterpiece of interiors behind a plain battlemented box.
facts: Built on the site of Syon Abbey, a Bridgettine monastery founded in 1431 and dissolved by Henry VIII in 1539; the present house grew from the converted abbey buildings.

Henry VIII's fifth wife Catherine Howard was held here in late 1541 before her execution, and in 1547 the King's own coffin, resting at Syon overnight en route to Windsor, reportedly burst, with dogs found licking the remains the next morning.
It has been a seat of the Percy family, Dukes of Northumberland, since 1594 and remains the family's London residence.
Robert Adam remodelled the state rooms from 1762; the Great Hall, Ante Room and Long Gallery are regarded as the launch of the 'Adam style', set in a park landscaped by Capability Brown.
The stone Percy Lion standing over the entrance front was moved here in 1874 from the demolished Northumberland House at Charing Cross.
legend: On 9 July 1553 Lady Jane Grey is traditionally said to have been offered the crown at Syon House, beginning her nine-day reign.

sources.note: Coordinates 51.4768, -0.3115; abbey founding 1431, Catherine Howard, Henry VIII coffin episode, Adam interiors, Percy ownership
Official site: Percy family seat, Adam interiors, Capability Brown park
Grade I listing for Syon House, confirming location and architectural history

w: The last great ducal house in London still in family hands, hiding Robert Adam's first masterpiece behind a plain battlemented box. Built on a dissolved 1431 abbey, Percy seat since 1594. Catherine Howard was held here; the story goes Henry VIII's coffin burst overnight at Syon, dogs found licking the remains by morning.

hook: A sweeping glasshouse with a soaring central dome, claimed as the first large-scale building of metal and glass, predating the Crystal Palace by a generation.
facts: Designed by Charles Fowler for the 3rd Duke of Northumberland and completed in 1827, built of gunmetal, Bath stone and glass with a 60-foot-high central glass dome.
It is widely cited as the first conservatory built from metal and glass on a large scale, and is said to have influenced Joseph Paxton's design for the 1851 Crystal Palace.
The curved wings sweep out to two pavilions; it once housed the Dukes' exotic plant collection within Syon's gardens.
It is Grade I listed and a popular wedding and film location set among the formal gardens and lake.

sources.note: Official site: Charles Fowler, 1827 completion, first large-scale metal-and-glass conservatory
Great Conservatory designed by Charles Fowler, completed 1827; within Syon Park gardens (~51.479, -0.309)
Historic England Grade I listing for the Great Conservatory at Syon Park

w: A sweeping dome of gunmetal and glass, finished 1827 -- claimed as the first large-scale metal-and-glass building, a generation before the Crystal Palace it's said to have inspired. Charles Fowler curved its wings out to two pavilions around the Dukes' exotics. Sixty feet of Bath stone and glazing, still standing among the lake and lawns.

hook: A willow-grown river island midstream off Brentford, once a barge yard, then a feral wilderness of rotting boats, now a working boatyard again.
facts: A roughly 1.7-acre island in the tidal Thames off Brentford, historically used for growing osiers (basket willows) and once known as Barbel Island for its fishing.
The Thames Lighterage Company set up a barge-building and repair yard here in the 1920s; at its peak it employed over 150 men, including women painters after WWII.
The yard closed in 1980 and the island reverted to wilderness, becoming a wildlife haven of naturalised willows and decaying hulls.
In January 2012 the Dahlia footbridge (designed by Beckett Rankine) linked the island to the Brentford bank, and John's Boat Works restarted boat-building for the first time in over 30 years.
The island and the neighbouring Brentford Ait sit in the river opposite Kew, with Brentford Ait planted up in the 1920s to screen the gasworks from Kew Gardens.

sources.note: Coordinates 51.48472, -0.29833; osier-growing, 1920s lighterage yard, 1980 closure, 2012 Dahlia Bridge and John's Boat Works
National Historic Ships: Lot's Ait boatyard history and revival

w: A willow island midstream off Brentford -- osier beds and barbel fishing, then a barge yard working 150 men, then three decades of feral wilderness and rotting hulls. In 2012 the Dahlia footbridge reached across and boat-building restarted. Its neighbour Brentford Ait was planted up to screen the gasworks from Kew.

hook: A green riverside terrace facing Kew Gardens across the Thames, laid out on the cleared ground of the old Brentford gasworks.
facts: The site of Watermans Park and the former arts centre was occupied by the Brentford Gasworks, where coal barges came up the Thames and gasholders dominated the skyline.
The Watermans Arts Centre opened on the site in 1984, its first theatre performance given by sitar player Ravi Shankar; the purpose-built centre had a 239-seat theatre, cinema and galleries.
The arts centre at 40 High Street closed on 11 April 2024 ahead of a planned move into central Brentford.
The park itself remains a riverside open space looking directly across to Kew Gardens, with moorings along the Thames frontage.

sources.note: Coordinates ~51.4857, -0.2987; gasworks site, opened with Ravi Shankar, 2024 closure of 40 High Street
Official Watermans site confirming Brentford riverside location and the move from the High Street building

w: A green riverside terrace facing Kew across the Thames, laid on the cleared ground of the old Brentford gasworks, where coal barges once unloaded under the gasholders. The 1984 arts centre opened with Ravi Shankar on sitar and ran cinema and theatre until 2024. The moorings and the long view to the Gardens remain.

hook: A squat granite pillar that boldly claims four of the most pivotal moments in English history all happened at this one Thames ford.
facts: A cylindrical Peterhead-granite monument, made partly from stone that was once part of Brentford Bridge, now standing outside the former Brentford County Court at the junction of the High Street and Alexandra Road.

Its four inscribed panels record the supposed crossing of the Thames by Julius Caesar in 54 BC, a church council called by King Offa of Mercia in 780/781, King Edmund Ironside's defeat of Cnut and the Danes in 1016, and the Civil War Battle of Brentford in 1642.
It was first erected around 1909 at Ferry Point, the river end of Ferry Lane, to mark the presumed site of the ford from which Brentford takes its name.
After being repeatedly buried under unloaded coal it was moved up Ferry Lane in the 1950s and relocated to its present High Street site in 1992.
legend: The claim that Julius Caesar forded the Thames here in 54 BC, supposedly confirmed by ancient wooden palisades found at Ferry Point, is local tradition rather than established fact; the true site of Caesar's crossing is disputed by historians.

sources.note: Description of the monument, its four events, Peterhead granite, and moves to the High Street outside the County Court
Local history society: detailed history of the monument and its relocations; confirms High Street / Alexandra Road location

w: A squat granite pillar that boldly pins four turning points of English history to one Thames ford: Caesar's crossing in 54 BC, Offa's church council of 781, Ironside routing Cnut in 1016, and the 1642 battle. Cut partly from the old Brentford Bridge. The Caesar claim, palisades and all, stays firmly in the realm of legend.

hook: A medieval Kentish-rag tower marooned beside a Georgian brick body, derelict for over half a century and the oldest standing structure in Brentford.
facts: A chapel and burial ground were founded on the site around 1175-1179 by Ralph de Brito; the present Great West Tower is 15th-century Kentish ragstone, built during the Wars of the Roses, and is the earliest surviving structure in Brentford.
The body of the church was rebuilt in brown brick in 1764; the building is Grade II* listed.
The church had close historic links with the Manor of Boston (Boston Manor), whose 1749 boundary defined the New Brentford parish.

It was closed for worship in 1961 and declared redundant in 1978; long left derelict, it sits on the Historic England Heritage at Risk Register and was acquired by Hounslow as part of Brentford's regeneration.

sources.note: Grade II* listing for the Church of St Lawrence, New Brentford; confirms 15th-century tower, 1764 brick body, High Street location
Local history society: founding c.1175, ragstone tower, Boston Manor links, closure and dereliction

w: A medieval Kentish-rag tower marooned beside a Georgian brick body -- Brentford's oldest standing structure, derelict for over half a century. The 15th-century Great West Tower went up during the Wars of the Roses; the church was rebuilt in brown brick in 1764, closed in 1961, and now sits on the Heritage at Risk register.

hook: West London's tidal gateway where the Grand Union Canal finally meets the Thames, the bottleneck through which 150 years of cargo squeezed in and out.
facts: Lock 101 on the Grand Union Canal and the canal's junction with the tidal Thames, marking the boundary between Port of London Authority and former British Waterways jurisdiction.
Originally built in 1805 to manage the tidal river, it became a notorious bottleneck before being enlarged and mechanised in 1962.
Because it is tidal, boat passages through Thames Lock must still be booked in advance with the Canal & River Trust.

Just upstream stands the double Gauging Lock (Brentford Lock, lock 100), built with a 1911 toll house where boats were 'gauged' (measured) to assess cargo tolls; it was once one of the busiest points on the whole Grand Union.

sources.note: Thames Lock No 101 gazetteer: location near Dock Road Bridge No 210, junction with the tidal Thames
Canal & River Trust: Thames Lock as West London's tidal gateway to the Thames; 1805 origin, 1962 enlargement

w: West London's tidal gateway, where the Grand Union Canal finally drops into the Thames -- the bottleneck 150 years of cargo squeezed through. Lock 101, first cut in 1805, mechanised in 1962, still tidal enough that passages must be booked. Just upstream the double Gauging Lock kept a 1911 toll house where boats were measured.

hook: The only view in England protected by its own Act of Parliament — the meadow loop of the Thames that Turner and Reynolds painted, deliberately frozen in time.
facts: The sweep of the Thames seen from the Terrace Walk at the summit of Richmond Hill is the only landscape view in England safeguarded by an Act of Parliament.
The protection came via the Richmond, Ham and Petersham Open Spaces Act 1902, passed after a public campaign halted a housing estate that threatened the foreground at Marble Hill.
J.M.W. Turner painted the scene in 'Thomson's Aeolian Harp' (1809), often called the most famous painting of the view.
Sir Joshua Reynolds painted 'The Thames from Richmond Hill' (1788) and kept a house, Wick House, on the hill, built for him by William Chambers.
The Terrace Walk promenade was laid out around 1700 and has been celebrated in literature and art ever since.
A telescope and a viewing panel near the boundary hedge identify landmarks; on a clear day Windsor Castle is visible.
w: The only view in England guarded by its own Act of Parliament -- the meadow loop of the Thames that Turner and Reynolds painted, frozen by the 1902 Act after a housing estate threatened the foreground. The Terrace Walk was laid out around 1700; on a clear day Windsor Castle shows on the horizon. A landscape kept deliberately still.

hook: A gleaming white Palladian villa built in the 1720s for a king's mistress — and a rare Georgian house designed for, and largely by, a woman.
facts: Marble Hill was built 1724-1729 as a Thames-side retreat for Henrietta Howard, mistress of George II and later Countess of Suffolk.
It is one of the earliest examples of Palladian architecture in England, designed by Roger Morris with input from Colen Campbell and Lord Herbert.
The grounds involved Alexander Pope and Charles Bridgeman; Pope and John Gay were neighbours, and Henrietta's circle was said to rival the royal court.
It is a relatively rare example of a substantial Georgian house built for and partly by a woman.
The villa was saved for the nation under the same 1902 Act that protected the view from Richmond Hill, after development threatened its setting.
It is now managed by English Heritage and is free to enter.
w: A gleaming white Palladian villa of the 1720s, built as a Thames retreat for Henrietta Howard, mistress of George II -- and rare for a grand Georgian house designed for and partly by a woman. Pope and Bridgeman shaped the grounds; the same 1902 Act that saved the Richmond view rescued its setting. Free to walk into, still.

hook: A Tudor gatehouse on Richmond Green still bearing Henry VII's restored coat of arms — the surviving doorway to the vanished palace where Elizabeth I died.
facts: The Gate House is one of only a few above-ground survivals of Richmond Palace, principal residence of Henry VII, Henry VIII and Elizabeth I.
Henry VII rebuilt the manor after a 1497 fire and renamed it Richmond Palace, after his earldom; the Gate House dates from around 1501.
The carved arms of Henry VII sit in a stone porch above the archway and were restored in 1976.
Other Tudor fragments survive nearby: the Wardrobe range on Old Palace Yard and elements within the later Trumpeters' House.
After the execution of Charles I, Parliament sold the palace in 1649 and most of it was demolished for building materials.
Street names — Old Palace Lane, Old Palace Yard — still map the lost complex; the Gate House is now a private residence (Grade I listed).
w: A Tudor gatehouse on Richmond Green, still carrying Henry VII's restored arms above the arch -- one of the few survivors of the vanished palace where Elizabeth I died. Henry rebuilt the manor after a 1497 fire and renamed it for his earldom around 1501. Parliament sold the rest in 1649 for building stone; the street names map what's gone.

hook: Frank Matcham's red-brick-and-terracotta jewel of 1899, reputed to be one of England's most haunted theatres.
facts: Richmond Theatre opened on 18 September 1899 (as the Theatre Royal and Opera House) with a performance of 'As You Like It'.
It was designed by the prolific Victorian theatre architect Frank Matcham, in red brick with buff terracotta.
It is Grade II* listed and regarded as one of the most completely preserved Matcham interiors in Greater London.
It stands at Little Green, on the edge of Richmond Green, and is now operated by ATG with around 840 seats.
It carries a long-standing haunted reputation, with staff reporting shifting boxes, scratch marks and lights turning on and off.
legend: The theatre is widely described as one of England's most haunted, with reported sightings and unexplained phenomena; these are local ghost stories rather than verified events.
w: Frank Matcham's red-brick-and-terracotta jewel of 1899, among the most completely preserved of his interiors left in London -- and, by long report, one of England's most haunted theatres. Staff tell of shifting boxes, scratch marks, lights flicking on alone. It opened with 'As You Like It' beside Richmond Green.

hook: The Thames-side pub that floods on a high spring tide — and keeps a working fireplace beneath a window, one of only a handful left in the country.
facts: The White Cross stands on the Richmond riverside off Water Lane and is Grade II listed (the building dates to 1838, on a site with a pub since around 1740).
Its Historic England listing singles out a stone-surrounded working fireplace placed unusually beneath a window, said to be one of very few still in use in Britain.
It is known locally as 'the pub that floods'; high spring tides cover the forecourt and approach, and the pub keeps wellington boots for customers.
An island servery bar serves the side rooms, including a mezzanine.
Local tradition holds the site was once occupied by a Franciscan friary, with remains reputedly in the cellars.
legend: It is locally said that the remains of a Franciscan friary lie beneath the pub in the cellars; this is unconfirmed pub lore.
w: The Richmond riverside pub that floods on a high spring tide -- it keeps wellington boots for drinkers when the forecourt goes under. Its listing singles out a working fireplace set, oddly, beneath a window, one of a handful left in use in Britain. Local lore puts a Franciscan friary's remains in the cellars; treat that as pub legend.

hook: A vast neoclassical pile at the top of Richmond Hill, built to nurse the most severely disabled soldiers of the First World War.
facts: The British Red Cross bought the old Star and Garter Hotel in 1915 to house severely disabled servicemen returning from WWI; it took its first residents on 14 January 1916.
The present building was constructed 1921-1924 to a design by Sir Edwin Cooper, developing a 1915 plan by Sir Giles Gilbert Scott, with capacity for about 180 men.
It was formally opened by George V and Queen Mary on 10 July 1924 and dedicated as the Women of the Empire's Memorial of the Great War.
The site had been an inn and then a grand Victorian hotel — the Star and Garter — which gave its name to the home and closed in 1906.
The building is Grade II listed and has since been converted into private residences.
w: A vast neoclassical pile crowning Richmond Hill, raised 1921-24 to nurse the most severely disabled soldiers of the Great War. The Red Cross bought the old Star and Garter hotel for the purpose in 1915; George V opened the present building in 1924 as the women of the Empire's war memorial. Now carved into private flats, the name kept.

hook: A ramshackle Eden of glasshouses and trailing plants down a Petersham lane, where the café once held a Michelin star among the terracotta pots.
facts: Petersham Nurseries was carved out of the grounds of Petersham House in the 1970s and bought by Gael and Francesco Boglione, who have lived in the adjoining house since 1997.
After restoration it reopened in 2004, with greenhouses refurbished and plastic replaced by terracotta and coir.
Chef Skye Gyngell developed its greenhouse restaurant, which won a Michelin star in 2011.
It is reached down Church Lane, the same lane that leads to St Peter's Church, Petersham.
The business later expanded with a Covent Garden outpost opened in April 2018, but the original remains in Petersham.
w: A ramshackle Eden of glasshouses and trailing plants down a Petersham lane, where the greenhouse cafe won a Michelin star in 2011 among the terracotta pots. The Bogliones carved it from the grounds of Petersham House and reopened it in 2004, swapping plastic for clay and coir. Reached down the same lane as St Peter's church.

hook: A 163-foot Chinese pagoda rising from a Surrey botanic garden, its eaves once guarded by eighty gilded dragons that vanished for two centuries.
facts: Designed by Sir William Chambers and completed in 1762 as a surprise present for Princess Augusta, founder of the gardens; it is the tallest and most ambitious of a 'royal circuit' of garden buildings showing world architectural styles.
The octagonal tower stands roughly 50 m (163 ft) high across ten storeys, with 253 steps to the viewing gallery.
Eighty wooden, gilded and polychrome dragons originally adorned the eaves; rotting, they were removed during repairs in 1784 and not replaced for over 230 years.
Historic Royal Palaces restored the building and reinstated 72 dragons in 2018, carving the lowest tier in wood like the originals and 3D-printing the upper tiers for structural reasons.
It is a Grade I listed building managed by Historic Royal Palaces within the Kew Gardens UNESCO World Heritage Site.
legend: A persistent local tale holds that the original gold-leaf dragons were sold off by a cash-strapped George IV to cover gambling debts; there is no documentary evidence, and the accepted explanation is simply that the timber dragons rotted.

sources.note: Coordinates 51.4713, -0.2957; Chambers, 163 ft, ten storeys, 253 steps, present for Princess Augusta
Historic Royal Palaces official page: completed 1762, eighty dragons, 2018 restoration with carved and 3D-printed dragons
Confirms 1762 completion, dragons removed 1784, restoration detail

w: A 163-foot Chinese pagoda rising over a botanic garden, its eaves once guarded by eighty gilded dragons that vanished for 230 years. Chambers built it in 1762 as a surprise for Princess Augusta; the rotting dragons came down in 1784, and only 72 returned in 2018. The story that George IV pawned the gold ones stays a story.

hook: The neat Georgian church presiding over Kew Green, in whose raised churchyard two founding Royal Academicians lie a few yards apart.
facts: Founded and dedicated on 12 May 1714 on land granted by Queen Anne; enlarged in 1770 to a design by Joshua Kirby at the expense of George III, who worshipped here from nearby Kew Palace.
Thomas Gainsborough (1727-1788), portrait and landscape painter, is buried in the churchyard in a Grade II* listed tomb; he asked to be laid near his friend Joshua Kirby.
Johan Zoffany (1733-1810), the German-born painter favoured by George III, is buried in a separate Grade II* listed tomb in the same churchyard.
Both Gainsborough and Zoffany were founder members of the Royal Academy in 1768.
The church itself is Grade II* listed (designated 10 January 1950) and forms the focal point of Kew Green.

sources.note: Coordinates 51.4838, -0.2879; founded 1714, enlarged 1770, Gainsborough and Zoffany graves, Grade II* listing
Official parish history: 1714 dedication, royal patronage, artist burials
Historic England Grade II* listing for the church confirming location and date

w: The neat Georgian church presiding over Kew Green, in whose raised churchyard two founding Royal Academicians lie yards apart: Gainsborough, who asked to rest near his friend Kirby, and Zoffany. Founded on Queen Anne's grant in 1714 and enlarged at the expense of George III, who worshipped here from Kew Palace nearby.

hook: A Kew tea room baking a curd tart whose recipe is supposedly so prized that Henry VIII locked it away at Richmond Palace.
facts: Newens 'The Original Maids of Honour' is a tea room and bakery at 288 Kew Road, opposite the Royal Botanic Gardens; the Newens family began trading the tart in 1850 and moved the business to Kew in 1860.
The maids of honour tart is a small puff-pastry shell filled with sweetened cheese curd, a traditional English confection associated with the Richmond and Kew area.
Newens has kept its version of the recipe secret since at least 1957, reputedly held in a locked box.
The bakery has continued the craft on the same Kew site for over 150 years.
legend: Tradition claims the tarts got their name when Henry VIII discovered Anne Boleyn and other maids of honour eating them at the royal household at Richmond, loved them so much that he confined the women and kept the recipe under lock and key at Richmond Palace; this is a colourful folk tale rather than documented history.

sources.note: Official site: Newens family, Tudor legend, Kew location, 1850/1860 dates, secret recipe
Puff pastry and curd composition, Richmond/Kew association, Newens history
OpenStreetMap geocode of 288 Kew Road, Kew TW9 3DU: 51.4807, -0.2883

w: A Kew tea room baking a curd tart so prized, the story runs, that Henry VIII locked the recipe away at Richmond Palace after catching his maids of honour eating it. The Newens family have kept their puff-pastry version secret since at least 1957, reputedly in a locked box, on the same Kew Road site for over 150 years.

hook: A riverside pub whose frontage notice insists Oliver Cromwell escaped through a tunnel to the island now bearing his name.
facts: A Grade II listed riverside public house at 15 Strand-on-the-Green, Chiswick; the present building is 18th-century with later additions and was licensed by at least 1722.
It is a two-storey, white-painted brick building retaining a pantile roof and two dormer windows, with a warren of small rooms on different levels.
A notice on the Thames-side frontage states that Oliver Cromwell was a frequent visitor because his sister, the Countess of Fauconberg, lived nearby.
Actor Donald Pleasence lived in the house at the end of the Bull's Head terrace, commemorated by a blue plaque.
The wooded eyot in the river opposite is officially Oliver's Island (Oliver's Eyot).
legend: An unsubstantiated legend says Cromwell stayed at the pub during the Civil War and escaped down a tunnel to hide on the river island now called Oliver's Island; historians note there is no reliable evidence for either the visit or the tunnel.

sources.note: Coordinates 51.484389, -0.278167; Grade II listing, 18th-century, licensed by 1722, Cromwell legend, Donald Pleasence plaque
Corroborates Strand-on-the-Green location, Cromwell legend and Oliver's Island story

w: A white-painted riverside warren at Strand-on-the-Green whose frontage notice swears Oliver Cromwell slipped out through a tunnel to the island now bearing his name. Licensed by 1722, a maze of little rooms. Donald Pleasence lived at the end of the terrace, blue-plaqued. The Cromwell tunnel, historians shrug, is folklore.

hook: A medieval Thames-side tavern with a watertight bulkhead door against the tide, named for the Lord Mayor's barge once moored at its steps and a fleeting star of a Beatles film.
facts: A riverside pub at 27 Strand-on-the-Green, Chiswick (W4 3PH), with origins traced to 1484 when it was reputedly called the Navigators Arms, later the Bohemia Head and the City Navigation Barge.
Its name derives from the City State Barge, the Lord Mayor of London's ceremonial barge, which was moored nearby.
Because the Thames is still strongly tidal here (rising and falling around 7 m), the old riverside bar is protected by a steel, ship-style bulkhead flood door.
The pub appears in the Beatles' 1965 film 'Help!', filmed partly at Strand-on-the-Green.

sources.note: 1484 origin, Navigators Arms / Bohemia Head / City Navigation Barge names, Lord Mayor's barge, tidal flood and bulkhead door
Strand-on-the-Green location, history and Help! film connection
Address and geocode 27 Strand on the Green W4 3PH: approx 51.4850, -0.2791

w: A Thames-side tavern traced to 1484, named for the Lord Mayor's ceremonial barge once moored at its steps -- and fitted with a ship's steel bulkhead door against a tide that rises seven metres at the wall. The old riverside bar floods without it. The Beatles filmed part of 'Help!' here at Strand-on-the-Green in 1965.

hook: A grand riverside inn at Isleworth Stairs, named for City apprentices who rowed upstream on their day off, with a smugglers' tunnel said to run to the church next door.
facts: A Grade II* listed pub at 62 Church Street, Old Isleworth; the present building dates to the early 18th century and was recorded as a licensed inn by 1731, on a site used as an inn far earlier.
It overlooks Isleworth Stairs, established in the reign of Henry VIII for the ferry that connected Richmond Palace with the north bank of the Thames.
Its name is traditionally explained by London apprentices rowing up the Thames to relax here after serving their masters.
The pub adjoins All Saints Church, whose riverside churchyard sits immediately to the north.
legend: A long-told legend claims a now-blocked tunnel runs from the pub's cellars to neighbouring All Saints Church, used by smugglers to move contraband; the pub is also said to have hosted Henry VIII, Charles I and Charles II, claims that belong to folklore rather than record.

sources.note: Coordinates 51.471124, -0.320870; 62 Church Street, Grade II* listing, early 18th century, licensed by 1731, Isleworth Stairs
Name origin (apprentices rowing upstream), riverside history, smugglers' tunnel legend

w: A grand riverside inn at Isleworth Stairs, named for the City apprentices who rowed up the Thames on their day off. Licensed by 1731, it overlooks the ferry steps that once linked Richmond Palace to the north bank. The long-told legend runs a smugglers' tunnel from the cellars to All Saints next door -- or just a good story.

hook: A Regency mansion that was Princess Amelia's pleasure ground and then the Rothschilds' suburban seat, its grounds still scattered with temple, orangery and boating pond.
facts: Princess Amelia, daughter of George II, bought the estate in 1760 as a country retreat and made it famous for her parties and political intrigues until her death in 1786.

Nathan Mayer Rothschild's family acquired the estate from 1835 and employed Sidney Smirke to improve the house and add an orangery; they reunited the divided estate by buying the Small Mansion in 1889.
An 18th-century garden temple by Sir William Chambers survives in the park, alongside the orangery and an ornamental boating pond.
Ealing and Acton councils bought the 200-acre estate in 1925 for public use; Gunnersbury Park opened to the public on 21 May 1926.
The Large Mansion opened as Gunnersbury Park Museum in 1929, displaying the local history of Ealing and Hounslow.
The mansion and park are on the Historic England register, the park listed Grade II*.

sources.note: Official history: Princess Amelia 1760, Rothschild ownership 1835, temple, orangery, public opening 1926, museum 1929
Rothschild Archive: Nathan Mayer Rothschild acquisition, Smirke improvements
Historic England registered park entry confirming location and history

w: A Regency mansion that was Princess Amelia's pleasure ground, then the Rothschilds' suburban seat, its grounds still scattered with a Chambers temple, an orangery and a boating pond. Amelia's parties and intrigues ran until 1786; the Rothschilds reunited the estate by 1889. Ealing and Acton bought the 200 acres for the public in 1925.

hook: A 17-metre aluminium lattice in a wildflower meadow that glows and hums in real time to the activity of a living Kew beehive.
facts: Designed by artist Wolfgang Buttress, The Hive is a roughly 17 m (56 ft) tall, 40-tonne structure assembled from about 170,000 aluminium parts to evoke a swarm.
It was first built as the centrepiece of the UK Pavilion at Expo 2015 in Milan, where it won a gold award, before being re-erected at Kew.
It was installed at Kew Gardens in June 2016, standing in a wildflower meadow near the Princess of Wales Conservatory.
Around 1,000 LED lights and accompanying sound respond in real time to vibration data from a live beehive in the gardens, with the audio pitched in the key of C.
Its reassembly marked the first time a UK Expo pavilion had been brought back and rebuilt in the UK.

sources.note: Official Kew page: Wolfgang Buttress, 17 m structure, live-beehive-driven light and sound, location near Princess of Wales Conservatory
Expo 2015 origin, 170,000 aluminium parts, June 2016 Kew installation, LED and sound system

w: A 17-metre aluminium lattice standing in a Kew wildflower meadow, glowing and humming in real time to a living beehive -- a thousand LEDs and a soundtrack pitched in C, driven by vibration data from the bees. Wolfgang Buttress built it from 170,000 parts as the UK's gold-winning pavilion at Milan's 2015 Expo, re-raised here in 2016.
